title: Launch Operations Engineer
setting: Cape Canaveral Space Force Station / Kennedy Space Center
companySize: Varies — SpaceX, ULA, Rocket Lab, or NASA contractor
description: You are a launch operations engineer responsible for getting rockets safely from the integration facility to orbit. Your work is the final link in the chain — if the vehicle does not launch successfully, nothing else matters. You manage launch pad systems, conduct countdown procedures, and make real-time decisions under extreme time pressure. Launch day is the most intense day in aerospace engineering.

schedule.activity: L-8 hours. Arrive at the launch control centre for a dawn launch. Begin pre-launch briefing. Weather is marginal — upper-level winds are at the limit. Go/no-go poll in 30 minutes.
schedule.note: Launch days start before dawn. You will have been up since 3 AM.

schedule.activity: Conduct go/no-go poll. Range safety is green. Weather is yellow — watching the wind. Vehicle systems are green. Propellant loading will begin on schedule.
Begin propellant loading. Monitor RP-1 and LOX fill levels, tank pressurisation, and vent valve operations. Watch for any anomalies in the propellant conditioning system.
schedule.note: Loading 500 tonnes of cryogenic propellant is a choreographed ballet of valves, sensors, and timing.

schedule.activity: T-1 hour. All propellant loaded. Conduct systems checks: flight computers powered, telemetry links verified, range tracking acquired. Run through abort procedures one final time.
T-30 minutes. Final weather check — winds have dropped. Green across the board. Begin terminal count. Transfer to internal power. Retract crew access arm (if crewed mission).
T-5 minutes. Autosequence initiated. The computer takes over final countdown. Monitor engine chill, pressurisation, and ignition sequence. Heart rate elevated.
schedule.note: The last 5 minutes are automated. Your job now is to watch for anomalies and call an abort if needed.

schedule.activity: T-0. LIFTOFF. Nine engines ignite. Vehicle clears the tower. Monitor ascent telemetry. Call out milestones: Max-Q, MECO, stage separation, SECO, orbit insertion. NOMINAL.
schedule.note: The 8 minutes from liftoff to orbit are the most intense minutes in engineering.

schedule.activity: Orbit confirmed. First stage has landed on the droneship. Mission success. Debrief begins. Review any anomalies noted during countdown and ascent.
Post-launch review meeting. Walk through every hold, anomaly, and close call. Document lessons learnt. Begin planning for the next launch in 10 days.
Afternoon: inspect the launch pad for any damage from the firing. Check hold-down clamps, flame trench, and propellant systems. Begin pad turnaround.
Head home. Decompress. Next launch is in 10 days — the turnaround cycle starts immediately.

responsibilities: Manage launch pad systems and ground support equipment
Conduct countdown procedures and real-time anomaly assessment
Coordinate between vehicle, range, weather, and payload teams
Maintain and refurbish launch pad between missions
Develop and refine launch procedures and checklists
Train for and respond to emergency scenarios (pad abort, propellant spills)
challenges: Extreme responsibility — your decisions directly affect crew safety and mission success
Irregular hours — launches happen when orbital mechanics dictates, not when it is convenient
Weather delays and scrubs are frustrating but necessary
Working with cryogenic propellants and high-pressure systems is inherently dangerous
Living in the Cape Canaveral area (limited by geography)
rewards: The visceral thrill of watching a rocket you helped prepare thunder off the pad
Clear, immediate feedback — the mission either works or it does not
Being the last link in the chain — your team is the reason the mission actually flies
SpaceX's rapid launch cadence means you see results frequently, not once per year
Unique engineering work that cannot be done remotely — you are physically at the pointy end
